title: Assignment Triage In 10 Minutes
content: List every assignment on one page, then mark each as due in 24 hours, due this week, or later. Start with one item from the 24-hour group and define a 15-minute first action.

title: Deadline Backward Planning
content: For each deadline, plan backward: draft date, outline date, reading date, and start date. This makes large work visible and lowers panic.

title: One Course At A Time Rule
content: When stress is high, choose one course block for the next 45 minutes and ignore all other classes. Single-course focus reduces switching cost and mental noise.

title: Start With The Rubric
content: Before writing, map your outline directly to rubric items. Aligning work to grading criteria prevents wasted effort and improves confidence.

title: Two-Hour Recovery Block
content: If you are behind, schedule one two-hour recovery block: 20 minutes plan, 80 minutes execution, 20 minutes submission or next-step setup.

title: Office Hours Message Template
content: Use a short message: 'I am working on X and stuck at Y. Could we discuss one question during office hours?' Specific questions get faster and better help.

title: Exam Study Prioritization
content: Prioritize high-yield topics first: past exam patterns, weak chapters, and problem types you miss often. Spend less time on topics you already handle well.

title: Five-Sentence Progress Note
content: At the end of each study block, write five sentences: what you started, what moved, what blocked you, what you will do next, and when you will resume.

title: Reading Compression Method
content: For long readings, extract only three things: key claim, supporting evidence, and one question. This keeps reading active and time-bounded.

title: Study Restart After Missed Day
content: After a missed day, avoid catch-up perfection. Do one 25-minute session and one admin task, then return to normal schedule the next day.

title: Application Funnel Diagnosis
content: Track counts at each stage: applied, replied, screened, interviewed, offered. The weakest stage tells you whether to improve targeting, resume, or interviewing.

title: Resume Bullet Upgrade
content: Rewrite bullets as action plus method plus measurable result. Concrete outcomes improve recruiter clarity and response rate.

title: Networking Follow-Up Script
content: Send a short follow-up within 48 hours: thank them, mention one insight, and ask one focused question. This keeps the relationship warm without pressure.

title: Job Targeting Narrow-Then-Expand
content: Start with 15 highly matched roles for two weeks, then expand once your materials improve. Early precision gives better feedback than mass applying.

title: Interview Story Bank
content: Prepare six reusable stories for conflict, leadership, failure, ambiguity, achievement, and teamwork. Rehearsed stories reduce anxiety and improve consistency.

title: Rejection Debrief Loop
content: After each rejection, log role type, stage reached, and one hypothesis for improvement. Review patterns weekly and make one change at a time.

title: Cold Outreach Framework
content: Use a three-line message: context, specific interest, and a small ask. Low-friction asks like 10-minute advice calls get more replies than referral requests first.

title: Weekly Search Sprint
content: Set a weekly sprint with fixed slots for applications, networking, and interview practice. Separate these activities so each gets focused attention.

title: Portfolio Proof Points
content: For each project, show problem, approach, and measurable outcome in plain language. Hiring managers evaluate evidence, not just tool lists.

title: Referral Timing Strategy
content: Ask for referral after a short value-building interaction, not in first contact. Share role link and one-sentence fit statement to make it easy to support you.

title: Conflict Cooling-Off Window
content: If voices rise, pause the conversation and schedule a specific time to revisit the issue. A planned pause often prevents escalation and personal attacks.

title: Use Observation Not Accusation
content: Start with observed facts and impact: 'The dishes stayed overnight and I felt stressed in the morning.' This is easier to hear than blame language.

title: Boundary Script For Shared Space
content: Define boundary as behavior plus time plus consequence: what is needed, by when, and what you will do if it is not met. Keep wording neutral and specific.

title: One-Issue Meeting Rule
content: Discuss one issue per conversation instead of stacking grievances. Narrow scope improves the chance of agreement and follow-through.

title: Temporary Space Strategy
content: If home tension is intense, use temporary buffers like library hours, a friend visit, or quiet workspace blocks. Space can reduce reactivity and protect functioning.

title: Repair Attempt Sentence
content: Try one repair line: 'I want us to solve this, not win this.' Repair attempts lower defensiveness and reopen collaboration.

title: Shared Agreement Checklist
content: Write brief agreements on noise, cleaning, guests, and bills in one shared note. Visible agreements reduce repeated arguments.

title: Escalation Safety Threshold
content: If conflict includes threats, intimidation, or fear, prioritize safety over negotiation. Contact trusted support, housing staff, or emergency resources as needed.

title: Consistent Wake Time Reset
content: Keep wake time stable every day for one week, even after a poor night. A fixed wake anchor helps reset sleep pressure faster than chasing perfect bedtime.

title: Evening Wind-Down Sequence
content: Use a 30-minute wind-down: dim lights, light stretch, and offline activity. Repeating the same pre-sleep cues helps your body transition to rest.

title: Worry Scheduling Method
content: Schedule 15 minutes of worry time earlier in the evening and write concerns with one next action each. This can reduce rumination at bedtime.

title: Bed Is For Sleep Rule
content: If awake in bed for a long period, get up for a calm low-light activity and return when sleepy. This rebuilds the bed-sleep association.

title: Late Caffeine Cutoff
content: Set a caffeine cutoff at least 8 hours before bedtime. Hidden caffeine in tea, soda, and energy drinks can quietly worsen sleep latency.

title: Nighttime Thought Parking
content: Keep a notepad by bed to park unfinished thoughts in one sentence. Externalizing tasks can reduce the urge to mentally rehearse them repeatedly.

title: Morning Light Exposure
content: Get outdoor light soon after waking for 10 to 20 minutes. Morning light helps stabilize circadian rhythm and supports earlier sleep onset.

title: Sleep Recovery After Bad Night
content: After a poor night, keep core schedule and reduce pressure to perform perfectly. A normal routine with short breaks usually recovers faster than oversleeping.

title: Two-Criteria Decision Grid
content: Choose two core criteria first, then score each option from 1 to 5. Simple scoring reduces emotional overload and makes tradeoffs visible.

title: Reversible First Step
content: When uncertain, pick a reversible test for one option this week. Action generates information that overthinking cannot provide.

title: Best-Case Worst-Case Most-Likely
content: Write best-case, worst-case, and most-likely outcomes for each option. Include one mitigation for worst-case to reduce fear-driven paralysis.

title: Deadline For Choosing
content: Set a decision deadline and define what information you will accept before it. Endless information gathering often delays progress without improving quality.

title: Values Alignment Check
content: Rank options by alignment with your top three values, not just short-term comfort. Values alignment improves long-term satisfaction and follow-through.

title: Ask One Trusted Reviewer
content: Ask one trusted person to stress-test your top option and your assumptions. One sharp review is often better than many mixed opinions.

title: Name The Thinking Pattern
content: Label the pattern before reacting, such as catastrophizing or all-or-nothing thinking. Naming a pattern creates distance and makes reframing easier.

title: Evidence For And Against
content: Write one belief, then list evidence for and evidence against it. Balanced evidence often softens extreme conclusions.

title: Self-Compassion Language Shift
content: Replace global labels like 'I am a failure' with specific statements about the situation. Precision reduces shame and protects motivation.

title: Emotion-To-Need Mapping
content: Translate emotion into need: anxiety may signal need for certainty, anger may signal need for boundaries, sadness may signal need for connection.

title: Micro-Recovery During Stress
content: Use 90-second micro-recovery breaks: slow exhale, shoulder release, and one grounding cue. Small resets can prevent stress buildup across the day.

title: Trigger Log For Patterns
content: Track trigger, thought, emotion intensity, and response for one week. Pattern data helps you choose targeted coping strategies.

title: Future Uncertainty Reframe
content: Shift from 'I must solve everything now' to 'I can handle the next meaningful step today.' This keeps action possible under uncertainty.

title: Coping Ladder Design
content: Build a three-level coping ladder: very easy, moderate, and stronger actions. Start at the easiest level to regain momentum when capacity is low.

title: Low-Pressure Check-In Text
content: Use a low-pressure text: 'I had a rough day and could use a short check-in this week.' Clear, small asks are easier for others to accept.

title: Support Circle Mapping
content: Map three support layers: close contacts, practical helpers, and professional resources. Knowing who to contact reduces delay during hard moments.

title: Ask For Specific Help
content: Specific requests work better than general distress statements. Ask for one concrete thing, such as a call, accountability check, or review of one document.

title: Reduce Isolation With Routines
content: Add one recurring social anchor each week, such as study group, gym class, or community event. Predictable contact lowers isolation risk.

title: High-Risk Signal First Response
content: If someone expresses hopelessness, desire to disappear, or self-harm thoughts, prioritize immediate safety. Encourage contacting trusted people now and emergency services if danger is near.

title: No-Isolation Safety Step
content: During elevated risk, avoid being alone when possible. Ask the person to stay with trusted support in person or on the phone.

title: Means Safety Reminder
content: When risk is high, encourage reducing access to items that could be used for self-harm and moving to a safer environment with support.

title: Emergency Escalation Rule
content: If there is immediate intent, plan, or inability to stay safe, escalate to local emergency resources immediately. Safety takes priority over normal coaching steps.

title: Safety Plan Minimum Elements
content: A minimal safety plan includes warning signs, two coping actions, two people to contact, and local crisis or emergency options. Keep it simple and visible.

title: Supportive Language Under Risk
content: Use calm validating language and avoid dismissing distress. Pair empathy with direct safety actions so support is compassionate and practical.
